segments.s020-g89: به سر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'onto the head' (به here marks direction onto); بر نهاد is a classical/periphrastic past (بر + نهاد, past 3rd sg. of نهادن) meaning 'placed on/put'. افسر تازیان is a noun + genitive (helmet of the Arabs) with the genitive formed by placing the plural noun تازیان after افسر (no modern ezāfe). The subject is omitted and understood from prior context (third person).
segments.s020-g90: بر ایشان marks the target/beneficiary ('upon them'); ببخشید is the past 3rd singular of بخشیدن used in the sense 'bestowed/gave' (not 'forgave'). سود و زیان is the compound direct object 'gains and losses' (or 'benefit and harm') placed after the verb; again the subject is omitted and context supplies 'he'.
segments.s020-g1: This is a simple past existential clause: 'یکی' (a certain) + noun 'مرد' (man) + past copula 'بود' (was) followed by the locative preposition 'اندر' (in) and the demonstrative 'آن' (that) with 'روزگار' (time/age). Word order is subject — copula — locative, typical in older Persian: 'There was a man in that time.'
segments.s020-g2: This is a noun phrase describing the man: 'ز' is the poetic genitive/partitive 'of/from', linking to 'دشت سواران' (riders of the plain) and the compound 'نیزه گذار' (one who bears/wields a spear). The phrase is verbless and appositional to the previous clause: 'a lancer of the plain's horsemen.'
segments.s020-g3: 'گرانمایه' is an epithet meaning 'esteemed/precious' and is followed by the correlative pair 'هم ... و هم ...' (both ... and ...) before the nouns 'شاه' (king) and 'نیک‌مرد' (good/noble man). Grammatically this phrase functions as a participial/attributive comment on the subject introduced earlier: 'esteemed by both the king and the good men.'
segments.s020-g4: This adverbial phrase begins with the poetic/causal preposition 'ز' (from/because of) + noun 'ترس' (fear) and the agent/target 'جهاندار' (the world-ruler/sovereign). 'با' here means 'with' and introduces 'باد سرد' literally 'cold wind' used metaphorically for a cold or stern demeanor. The whole segment gives the reason or manner: 'from fear of the sovereign, with a cold wind (i.e. a cold manner).' Note the poetic metaphorical use of 'باد سرد.'
segments.s020-g5: The relative particle 'که' ('who/that') introduces a clause identifying the man: proper name 'مرداس' followed by 'نام' ('named'/'name') and the epithet 'گرانمایه' (esteemed), with the past copula 'بود' (was). Word order is subject (name) + predicative noun phrase + copula, giving 'who was named Mardas, esteemed.'
segments.s020-g6: 'به' is a prepositional marker meaning 'in/regarding' and governs the coordinated nouns 'داد و دهش' (justice and giving/generosity). 'برترین' (most/excellent) modifies 'پایه' (rank/standing), and the clause closes with the past copula 'بود' (was). Together it reads 'in justice and generosity he was of the foremost rank.' Note 'دهش' is a noun here meaning the act/quality of giving (generosity).
segments.s020-g7: This phrase uses the archaic/dative particle 'مر' (to/for me/him — here marking possession or benefit) together with 'او را' (him; 'او' with the object/topic marker 'را') to indicate possession or relation: 'to him.' 'ز' introduces the type 'دوشیدنی چارپای' ('milchable quadrupeds' — animals that can be milked). The construction thus means roughly 'to him belonged milch quadrupeds.'
segments.s020-g8: 'ز' again is partitive 'of/from', 'هر' = 'each', and 'آمدندی' is an archaic past-plural form meaning 'they came' (third-person plural past). 'به' = 'to' and 'جای' = 'place/stead' — together 'from each one a thousand came to (his) place.' The verb 'آمدندی' carries the past-tense action and matches the plural subject implied by 'هزار' readings of the distribution.
segments.s020-g17: The phrase follows a classical Persian order: 'X را نام Y بود' = 'X's name was Y' (literally: 'to the world‑seeker the name Zahhak was'). 'را' marks the preceding noun as definite/topic. 'بود' is the past copula ('was').
segments.s020-g18: Three adjectival predicates are joined by 'و' and closed by the past copula 'بود' — typical predicative coordination: 'he was brave and light‑headed and impure/corrupt.' Each adjective modifies the subject (Zahhak) introduced in the previous clause.
segments.s020-g19: 'کجا' here means 'where(ever)'; 'بیور اسپش' is a name + possessed noun (اسپش = 'his steed/horse') and functions as the object of naming. 'همی خواندند' is a habitual past ('they used to call/sing/call out'), with 'همی' marking repeated or customary action and 'خواندند' the past plural.
segments.s020-g20: 'چنین نام بر پهلوی راندند' places the name ('چنین نام') before the verb in typical Persian order; 'بر پهلوی' = 'on the flank/side' (پهلوی = side). 'راندند' is past plural of راندن; here it functions idiomatically as 'they set/placed (or drove/inscribed) the name upon the flank.'
segments.s020-g21: 'کجا' = 'where(ever)'; the noun phrase 'بیور از پهلوانی شمار' uses 'از' to mark membership ('counted among') and 'شمار' ('count/number') to indicate being reckoned/numbered in the class 'پهلوانی' (heroism/champions). Together: 'wherever Bevor was reckoned among the heroes.'
segments.s020-g22: 'بود' is the past copula ('was'); 'بر زبان' literally 'upon the tongue' = 'on the lips / spoken', 'دری' specifies the language (Dari/Persian), and 'ده هزار' is a numeric intensifier ('ten thousand'), i.e. 'spoken many times' — so: 'his name was spoken in Dari ten thousand times.'
segments.s020-g23: The pair 'ز ... به ...' functions as a correlative range 'from ... to ...': 'from the Tazi (Arabian) steeds to the golden praise.' 'تازی' qualifies 'اسپان' (Arab/Arabian horses) and 'زرین ستام' means 'golden praise' (an elevated, poetic phrase).
segments.s020-g24: 'ورا بود بیور' is a concessive/locative construction meaning 'to him there was Bevor' i.e. 'Bevor possessed/held (it)'; the following relative clause 'که بردند نام' qualifies what that was: 'which they bore/took as a name.' 'بود' is past copula; 'که' introduces the clause; 'بردند' is past plural of بردن used here as 'they carried/took (the name)'.
segments.s020-g41: برین is an enclitic-like combination of بر + این (literary order: 'بر این') meaning 'upon/for this' and attaches to زمانه ('time/fate') which is the grammatical subject. خواجه is the noun 'khwāja / master' and سالخورد is a postpositive adjective 'aged, venerable' modifying خواجه. The whole segment reads literally 'time for this aged khwāja.'
segments.s020-g42: همی + دیر ماند is a literary/perfective-continuous phrase: همی (still/continually) + دیر ماند ('remains/lasts long'), giving 'continues to remain long'. تو is the second-person subject 'you'. اندر is the locative 'in/under', followed by نورد (here a poetic/obscure noun), so the clause means 'you remain a long time in/under N.'; the exact force of نورد is lexical/poetic and determines the precise nuance.
segments.s020-g43: بگیر is an imperative 'take!' governing the following noun phrase. این is the demonstrative 'this'. سر here functions as 'chief/top' (part of the compound sense 'chief/important item') and مایه‌ور is an adjectival compound meaning 'wealthy' (مایه = 'means/wealth' + ور = '-ful'). جاه 'rank/status' is the object of the command and او 'his/he' marks possession: 'take this wealthy man's chief rank.'
segments.s020-g44: تو را uses the topical/object marker را after the pronoun 'you' to mark the person for whom the action matters. زیبد is a stative/impersonal verb meaning 'befit' or 'suit' (here third-person singular impersonal: 'it suits'). اندر + جهان گاه او is a locative/time phrase 'in the world / in his day' (گاه = 'time/day/occasion') completing the thought: 'it will befit you in the world at his time.'
segments.s020-g45: بر این گفته من is a prepositional/topic phrase 'on/with this saying of mine' (بر این = 'with this', گفته = 'saying/statement', من = 'my'). چو داری وفا is a conditional clause: چو = 'if/when' here conditional 'if', داری 'you have/keep' (present), and وفا = 'fidelity/faithfulness' — together 'if you keep faith with this my saying.'
segments.s020-g46: جهاندار باشی is a result clause: جهاندار 'ruler/sovereign' followed by باشی, the second-person singular present/subjunctive of 'to be' used here as 'you shall be / you will be.' یکی پادشا is an apposition/emphasis: 'one king' or 'a certain king' (i.e. 'a monarch'). The structure: 'you will be a sovereign, a king.'
segments.s020-g47: چو here means 'when' introducing a temporal clause. ضحاک is the subject 'Zahhāk'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' and اندیشه کرد is a light-verb construction 'he made thought' = 'he thought / he pondered.' The sequence gives 'When Zahhāk heard, he thought / reflected.'
segments.s020-g48: ز خون پدر is an ablative phrase 'from/because of his father's blood' (ز = 'from/because of'). شد is the past of 'become' and takes the nominal predicate دلش پر ز درد: دل + ش (possessive suffix 'his') = 'his heart'; پر ('full (of)') governs ز درد 'with sorrow/pain'. The clause means 'his heart became full of pain/sorrow because of his father's blood.'
segments.s020-g33: جوان نیک‌دل گشت فرمانش کرد is two coordinated phrases: "جوان نیک‌دل گشت" uses گشت (past of گشتن) as a copular/transformative verb meaning "became/was (a) kind‑hearted youth." "فرمانش کرد" follows with فرمان + the enclitic pronoun -ش ("his/that/it") and کرد (did/made) meaning "he carried out/obeyed that command." Word order places the subject phrase first, then the clause describing the action (object+کرد).
segments.s020-g34: چنان چون بفرمود سوگند خورد: چنان چون + بفرمود is a circumstantial phrase "just as/he said/commanded" (بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb). سوگند خورد is an idiom meaning "to swear an oath" (خورد here functions idiomatically with سوگند). The clause links to the previous by showing the manner in which he acted.
segments.s020-g35: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); راز تو is a possessive noun phrase ('your secret'). با کس نگویم is negative first‑person present/subjunctive ('I will not tell to anyone'), with با کس = "to/with anyone." The final phrase ز بن is a poetic adverbial ('from the root' = 'in any way/at all') intensifying the negation.
segments.s020-g36: ز تو بشنوم هر چه گویی سخن: ز تو ('from you') marks source; بشنوم is first‑person present/subjunctive 'I shall/hear' (here promise to hear). هر چه introduces a free relative 'whatever/whichever (thing)'; گویی is the clause verb 'you say' (subjunctive/indicative form); سخن = 'words/speech' completes the idea. Word order is source → verb → free relative → object.
segments.s020-g37: بدو گفت is the ditransitive phrase 'he said to him' (بدو = to him/unto him before the verb). جز تو کسی کدخدای is an elliptic interrogative: جز تو ('except you') + کسی ('anyone') + کدخدای ('headman'), i.e. 'except you, who could be headman?' The order reflects classical Persian ellipsis of a copula/question particle.
segments.s020-g38: چه باید همی با تو اندر سرای is a rhetorical question: چه باید = 'what need/is there a need', همی adds emphasis ('indeed/just'), با تو اندر سرای = 'to be with you in the house' (با تو = with you; اندر سرای = in the house). باید expresses necessity/expectation rather than obligation here.
segments.s020-g39: چه باید پدر کش پسر چون تو بود: چه باید opens a rhetorical question ('what use/what need'); پدر کش پسر is a compound nominal phrase 'father‑killing son' (کش is the agentive element forming 'killer'); چون تو بود ('if he were like you' / 'if such as you was') uses بود (past/copula) to state the hypothetical quality.
segments.s020-g40: یکی پندت از من بباید شنود: یکی here is indefinite ('one/someone'); پندت ('your advice') is the noun with possessive -ت; از من ('from me') marks source; بباید (modal 'must/ought') + شنود (3rd‑person present 'he/one hears') yields 'someone ought to hear one of your counsels from me' — i.e. it is fitting that at least one of his counsels be heard via me. Word order is topical (یکی) → object (پندت از من) → modal+verb.
segments.s020-g57: بپرسید is an older/polished past (reported-speech) verb meaning 'he asked'. کاین is an archaic demonstrative ('this/that') placed before the noun چاره ('remedy/solution'). با من بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive-style sequence meaning 'say/tell (it) to me' where با = 'with/to' and من is the recipient; the verb form بگوی here is the quoted-speech form 'say!' (the listed senses did not include the verbal sense).
segments.s020-g58: نتابم is a negative first-person verb meaning 'I will not yield / I will not endure' (classical usage of تابیدن = 'to endure/submit' here). ز رای تو is a short prepositional phrase 'because of / by your counsel' (ز = 'from/by/owing to', رای = 'counsel/opinion', تو = 'you'), and من هیچ روی is emphatic: من is a topical/emphatic subject 'I', هیچ روی (literally 'no face') is an idiom 'in no way / not at all.'
segments.s020-g59: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him'); the reported speech follows: من is the speaker 'I', چاره سازم is a first-person present/future-like form 'I will make/devise a remedy' where سازم is 'I make/prepare', and ترا is the clitic object 'you' plus را (object marker) immediately after the verb (classical word order allows enclitic pronouns and the object marker together).
segments.s020-g60: به خورشید سر برفرازم ترا is a directional result clause: به خورشید = 'toward/to the sun' (به marks direction), سر = 'head' (the thing to be exalted), and برفرازم is a first-person verb meaning 'I will exalt/raise' (بر + فراز + ـم; an elevated/poetic verbal form). ترا (ت + را) again marks 'you' as the definite object. The whole means 'I will raise your head to the sun.'
segments.s020-g61: مر is an archaic dative/topic clitic meaning 'to me' or 'for me' placed before the rest of the clause; آن پادشا را is the definite/topic-marked noun phrase 'that king' (را is the emphatic/definite object/ topic marker). در اندر سرای is a pleonastic prepositional phrase 'in the inner house/palace' (در and اندر both mean 'in/within' in classical style).
segments.s020-g62: یکی = 'a certain' (indefinite emphasizing one), بوستان = 'garden', بود is the past copula 'was' (classical past), and بس دلگشای is an adjectival phrase where بس functions as an intensifier 'very/most' and دلگشای means 'heart-pleasing / delightful.' The predicate follows the subject in the classical order: 'there was a certain garden very delightful.'
segments.s020-g63: گرانمایه is an honorific adjective 'precious / noble' modifying شبگیر (a compound noun here meaning 'night-attendant' or 'one who watches/comes at night'). برخاستی is the classical past/preterite 'he rose' (simple past third person). The phrase describes a person of rank rising at night.
segments.s020-g64: ز بهر is a classical prepositional pairing meaning 'for the sake of / for the purpose of' (ز = 'from/because of', بهر = 'for'), پرستش = 'worship/adoration', and بیاراستی is a past verb 'he adorned/prepared' (poetic form of آراستن with the past-ending), so the clause means 'he prepared/arrayed himself for worship.'
segments.s020-g81: This is a verbless (or copular) clause typical of classical Persian: the prepositional phrase 'به خون پدر' ('in/with regard to the father's blood') is fronted as topic, followed by the past verb 'گشت' (became) and the predicate 'همداستان' (literally 'one of the story' → 'accomplice, in league'). The subject is omitted (understood from context). 'خون پدر' is a genitive construction 'blood of the father' often used to mean the shedding of the father's blood or the crime against the father.
segments.s020-g82: 'ز' is the archaic/preclassical preposition 'from' (poetic register). 'شنیدم' is the simple past first‑person 'I heard'; the pronoun 'من' follows the verb for emphasis (common in Persian poetry). 'این داستان' is the direct object 'this story/tale'. Literally: 'From a wise man I heard this story.'
segments.s020-g83: 'که' introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that' / 'when' / 'if'). 'فرزند بد‌گر شود' = 'the son becomes/of a bad turn' where 'بدگر' is an adjective/noun 'turned bad, vicious' and 'شود' is the present/subjunctive/presentive 'becomes'. 'نره شیر' ('male lion') stands as a concessive or descriptive phrase — 'even if he is a male lion' — with no copula; classical Persian often omits the copula in such apposition.
segments.s020-g84: The phrase 'به خون پدر' ('with regard to / in the matter of the father's blood') marks the domain (i.e., acts involving the father's blood). 'هم' here functions as 'even'/'also' (concessive emphasis). 'نباشد دلیر' uses the classical negative subjunctive/optative form 'نباشد' to mean 'is not' / 'will not be' brave; 'دلیر' is the predicate adjective 'brave'. Together: 'even regarding his father's blood he is not brave.'
segments.s020-g85: 'مگر' here means 'but rather' / 'however'. 'در نهانش' is 'in its hiddenness' or 'in secret' — note the enclitic '-ش' (= 'his/its') attached to 'نهان' referring back to the affair just mentioned. 'سخن دیگرست' is a fixed poetic formula: 'the story/talk is different' (with the archaic copula '-ست' = 'is').
segments.s020-g86: This line has an older word order: 'پژوهنده را' uses the object/ topical marker 'را' to mark the beneficiary/experiencer ('for the inquirer' / 'as for the seeker'). 'راز' is 'secret' and 'با مادرست' should be read as 'با مادر است' ('is with the mother') — in the manuscript the copula is contracted. So the whole segment means 'the secret for the inquirer is with the mother.'
segments.s020-g87: This is a sequence of epithets and a name in apposition: 'فرومایه' (base, low-born) qualifies 'ضحاک' (Zahhāk) and 'بیدادگر' is a descriptive nominative (tyrant/cruel one). Classical Persian often strings adjectives and names without copula; all three words describe the same person.
segments.s020-g88: 'بدین چاره' = 'by this expedient/stratagem' ('بدین' = 'by/with this', 'چاره' = 'means/expedient'). 'بگرفت' is the past tense 'he seized / he took hold of' (ب-گرفت). 'جای پدر' = 'the father's place/position' (i.e. the throne). The logical order is: 'By this device he seized the father's place.' Note that the tokenization in the source splits 'بدین' into parts; read them together as the prepositional phrase.
segments.s020-g73: به + چاه + اندر افتاد is a classical Persian construction meaning “fell into the well”: به here marks motion/toward (combined with اندر ‘in’), and افتاد is the past tense verb ‘fell/occurred’. و links the next verb; بشکست is a past intransitive/predicative verb ‘was broken/was dashed’. پست at the end is an adjectival predicate (‘low/humble’) that will be completed by the copula in the next segment.
segments.s020-g74: شد is the past copular/verb ‘became’ and takes the adjective pَِست from the previous segment: پست شد = ‘became low/humbled’. آن is the demonstrative ‘that’, and the three words نیک‌دل مرد یزدان‌پرست form a noun phrase (adjectives before the noun) = ‘that kind‑hearted, God‑worshipping man’. The copula that links predicate and subject is explicit (شد).
segments.s020-g75: به هر نیک و بد is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in every good and bad’ (i.e. in good times and bad). شاه is the subject. آزاد مرد is an attributive phrase where آزاد is an adjective ‘generous/noble’ and مرد (‘man’) is a substantive used to say ‘a man of that quality’; the copula ‘was’ is omitted (typical in poetry) and understood.
segments.s020-g76: This is an optative/wish: به فرزند بر نازده marks the target (‘upon the child, the pampered one’), where بر = ‘upon’ and نازده = ‘pampered/delicate’. باد is an optative particle here meaning ‘may there be’ (used to wish ill), and سرد is the predicate adjective ‘cold’. Together: ‘May a cold wind be upon the pampered son.’
segments.s020-g77: همی before the verb is a classical/perfective-continuous marker indicating habitual or ongoing past action. پروریدش is the past tense ‘he reared/nurtured him’ (object clitic -ش = ‘him’). به ناز و به رنج are instrumental/prepositional phrases ‘with pampering and with hardship’ describing the manner of upbringing.
segments.s020-g78: بدو is the indirect object ‘to him/for him’ placed before the verb for emphasis. بود شاد = ‘was happy/was pleased’ (بود = past copula). و links the clauses; بدو داد گنج = ‘and to him he gave treasure’ with داد as the past verb ‘gave’ and گنج the direct object.
segments.s020-g79: چنان is an adverb ‘thus/so’. بدگهر is an adjectival compound ‘evil‑mannered/ill‑disposed’ and شوخ here conveys ‘impudent/playful’ (given the negative context, ‘impudent’ is appropriate). فرزند او = ‘his son’ (possessive pronoun او follows the noun — a common classical order).
segments.s020-g80: بگشت is the past intransitive ‘turned/turned aside’ (past of برگشت/گشت); it takes an origin marked by از + noun. از ره داد = ‘from the path/way of justice’ (داد here is the noun ‘justice’). و پیوند او coordinates a second complement ‘and his bond/ties’, where او marks possession. Word order places the verb first (bending/poetic order) then the prepositional phrase(s).
segments.s020-g25: شب و روز (night and day) is a temporal frame placed before the verb. بودی is the second-person past form 'you were/had' (poetic past), and دو بهره به زین is the quantified object 'two shares (or turns) at the saddle' with the preposition به marking the locus ('at/to the saddle'). The order (time – verb – object) is a poetic, somewhat inverted order but reads as 'you had two shares at the saddle, night and day.'
segments.s020-g26: ز (poetic = از) introduces motive: 'from/out of.' ز روی بزرگی = 'from the face/disposition of greatness' i.e. 'out of magnanimity.' The contrastive phrase نه از روی کین uses نه + از to negate the alternate motive: 'not from the face/disposition of hatred.' Note روی here is idiomatic 'face/disposition' and بزرگی is used in the sense 'magnanimity.'
segments.s020-g27: چنان بد که is a standard result clause: 'so bad that...' که introduces the following clause. ابلیس is the subject, and روزی پگاه is the temporal phrase 'one day, at dawn' (روزی here ‘on a day/one morning’ or 'at daybreak') placed after the subject in poetic order.
segments.s020-g28: بیامد is the simple past 'he came.' به سان is a fixed simile 'in the manner of/like,' followed by یکی نیک‌خواه 'a certain well-wisher' (یکی here indefinite 'one/a certain'). The whole phrase means 'he came like a certain well-wisher' — i.e. pretending benevolence.
segments.s020-g29: دل مهتر is 'the heart of the leader/chief' (possessive apposition: possessed + possessor). از راه نیکی 'from the path of goodness' is the source phrase, and ببَرد (ببرد) is the past/action verb 'he carried away / led away.' The subject is omitted (contextually the deceiver), so the clause reads 'he carried away the chief's heart from the way of goodness.'
segments.s020-g30: جوان (the young man) is the subject. گوش ... سپرد is an idiom: 'to give ear' (i.e. to listen/attend). گفتار او را 'his speech' is the object (marked by را). سپرد is past 'he entrusted/committed' — together: 'the young man lent an ear to his speech and entrusted himself to it.'
segments.s020-g31: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'). پیمانت is 'your pledge' with the second-person suffix ت attached to پیمان. خواهم is the first-person auxiliary meaning 'I will' or 'I wish (to),' and نخست 'first' places the action in sequence: 'I will (take) your pledge first.' The verb گفت here introduces reported speech; note گفت in the wordlist lacks the 'said' sense so its sense index is unavailable.
segments.s020-g32: پس آنگه is a double sequential adverb 'then/and afterwards.' سخن برگشایم uses برگشایم (1st-person) 'I will open/reveal/unfold (my) speech' — here future/intentional sense in reported speech — and درست 'properly/correctly' qualifies how he will speak. The clause completes the quoted plan: first the oath, then the clear statement.
segments.s020-g9: همان is an emphatic demonstrative 'that same / the very'. گاو دوشا is a noun + postpositive adjective: گاو 'cow' + دوشا 'milker, milk‑giving' (i.e. a milking cow). به فرمانبری is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by/with obedience' where به is an adverbial/agentive preposition and فرمانبری is the noun 'obedience'. The whole segment lists the animal and qualifies it as obedient/for obedience.
segments.s020-g10: همان again 'that same' (back‑reference). تازی functions adjectivally as 'Arabian' modifying اسب 'horse/steed'. گزیده is a past participle/adjectival form 'chosen / select' placed after the noun (a common poetic order). مری specifies female: 'mare'. Together: 'that same chosen Arabian mare.'
segments.s020-g11: بز و میش is a simple coordinated noun phrase 'goats and ewes' (و = 'and'). شیرور is an adjective 'milk‑bearing / good at giving milk' modifying them; here the short word بد precedes شیرور as an archaic/decorative element forming the compound بد شیرور meaning something like 'well‑milking' (this use of بد is archaic and not the common 'bad'). همین/همچنین follows to mark 'likewise / similarly'.
segments.s020-g12: به دوشیزگان داده is a light clausal unit: به = 'to/for', دوشیزگان = 'the maidens', داده = 'given' (past participle or perfect). The final phrase بد پاکدین is ambiguous: پاکدین is the adjective/noun 'pious / pure‑religioned', while the intervening بد here is not the usual 'bad' sense and likely functions as an archaic connective or emphatic element (or may mark the agent); the whole therefore reads 'were likewise given to the maidens (by the pious / to the pious maidens)'.
segments.s020-g13: This unit contains a relative clause: کسی را که بودی نیاز = 'the person who was in need' (کسی را = 'someone' with the object/topic marker را; که بودی = relative 'who was'; نیاز = 'need'). به شیر at the start is best read as 'with regard to a lion' or 'if (one) needed a lion' (شیر here = 'lion' in epic usage). The phrase therefore frames a condition: 'if someone was in need of a lion...'.
segments.s020-g14: بدان is an archaic compound (بـ + آن) meaning 'to/at that', so بدان خواسته = 'at that request/wish'. دست بردی is a fixed verbal phrase 'to put forth/lay the hand on' (i.e. act upon, seize, or intervene) with بردی as past of بردن 'to take/lay'. فراز is an adverbial 'on high / up / forth' emphasizing the upward/decisive motion. Together: 'at that request you reached out your hand (and acted).' Note: دان here is not 'to know' but part of the archaic demonstrative compound.
segments.s020-g15: This noun group reads with poetic word order: پسر بد مر = 'a son hostile/bad toward me' (بد qualifies پسر and مر = 'to me' marks the person toward whom he is bad). این پاکدل را = 'of this pure‑hearted one' with را marking the affected/theme. یکی is the numeral 'one / a single'. The phrase sets up 'not even one son of that good‑hearted man was friendly to me.'
segments.s020-g16: کش here is an archaic/poetic form related to 'کس/کِس' (meaning 'anyone/one') or an agentive element; its exact lexical entry is uncertain in the supplied senses, so it is treated as idiomatic here. از = 'of/from', مهر = 'affection / love', بهره = 'share/portion', نبود = 'there was not' (past), اندکی = 'a little / even a small amount'. The clause thus says 'there was not even a little share of affection.'
segments.s020-g65: سر و تن is a standard paired phrase meaning 'head and body' (whole person), with و simply joining the two nouns. بشستی is a 2nd-person past form (archaic/classical narrative) meaning 'you sat' here; نهفته is an adjectival/past-participial form 'hidden' and the prepositional phrase به باغ (= 'in/to the garden') gives the location. Word order is topic (سر و تن) + verb (بشستی) + locative phrase (نهفته به باغ).
segments.s020-g66: پرستنده is the subject 'the worshipper/devotee'. با او is an instrumental/associative prepositional phrase 'with him', placed before the verb. ببردی is a 2nd-person past form 'you took (away)' used here in narrative sequence (classical/epic tense), and چراغ is the direct object 'the lamp' (object can follow the verb in these lines).
segments.s020-g67: بیاورد is 3rd-person past 'he/it brought' (narrative past). وارونه is an adjective/adverb 'inverted/upside-down' placed before the possessed noun phrase; ابلیس بند is a compound/possessive sense 'the devil's bond/snare' (ابلیس qualifying بند). Together the clause means 'he brought the devil's inverted snare.'
segments.s020-g68: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain' introducing the next item. ژرف modifies چاهی as 'deep'; چاهی here denotes 'a well' (the -ی form used in poetry). به ره is a locative 'into/on the road/way', and بر بکند is the verbal phrase (بر + بکند) meaning 'he dug/made' — the verb is 3rd-person past and completes the clause: 'a certain deep well he dug in the way.'
segments.s020-g69: پس is a connective 'then/after that'. ابلیس is the subject 'Iblis/the Devil'. وارونه here qualifies the following noun phrase 'آن ژرف چاه' ('that deep well') — the adjective-phrase order is somewhat flexible in classical lines, so the phrase reads 'then Iblis (regarding) that inverted deep well...'.
segments.s020-g70: به خاشاک پوشید is 'he covered (it) with chaff' — به marks the means/agentive instrument 'with/by' and پوشید is the 3rd-person past 'he covered'. و بسترد راه links the next verb: بسترد (past of ستر) 'he concealed/veiled' and راه is the object 'the road/path'. Both verbs agree with the earlier subject 'ابلیس'.
segments.s020-g71: سر here means 'chief/head' (title) and governs the following genitive/attributive nouns: تازیان 'of the Arabs' specifies whose chief he is. مهتر is a reinforcing title 'chief/elder', and نامجوی is a proper name (here the personal name 'Namjuy'). The sequence gives the person: 'Namjuy, the Arab chief.'
segments.s020-g72: شب آمد is a common classical phrasing 'night came' meaning 'he came by night' (آمد is 3rd-person past 'came'). سوی باغ is a directional phrase 'toward the garden'. بنهاد is 3rd-person past 'he put/placed' or 'directed', and روی 'face' here is the object of بنهاد: 'he set/turned his face toward the garden.' The verb+object idiom benahād + rūy = 'turned his face.'
segments.s020-g49: به ابلیس گفت is a prepositional indirect-object phrase + past verb: 'he said to Iblis' with the subject omitted (third person). این سزاوار نیست is the content clause (direct speech) where این 'this' points to the proposed action and سزاوار (adjective) is negated by نیست ('is not').
segments.s020-g50: دگر گوی is an imperative-like instruction: دگر here means 'no longer/anymore' and گوی is the classical/poetic 2nd-person imperative of گفتن ('say! / speak!'). کاین begins a clause meaning 'that this/that', and از در کار نیست is an idiomatic locution: از در (literally 'from the door/way') + کار ('matter, affair') + نیست → 'has no place in the affair / is not relevant'.
segments.s020-g51: بدو is 'to him' (indirect object). گفت‌گر here should be read as گفت + گر (classical 'if'), introducing a conditional ('he said: if ...'); the lexeme was given as 'speaker' but in context گر is the conditional particle. بگذری is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive (or jussive) of گذشتن 'to pass/let pass' meaning 'you let/forgive/overlook'. زین سخن = 'this speech/these words' (زین = 'from/over this' + سخن 'speech').
segments.s020-g52: بتابی is present (2nd-person) of تابیدن here used in the sense 'be affected/feel/ be moved' (classical usage allows present to imply future/result). ز introduces the cause/agent ('by/from'), سوگند و پیمان من = 'my oath and my pledge/covenant' (سوگند and پیمان linked by و). The clause warns of consequence: 'you will be affected by my oath and covenant.'
segments.s020-g53: بماند is third-person present/subjunctive of ماندن 'to remain' used as a jussive/result ('let it remain' / 'it will remain'). به گردنت literally 'on your neck' (به + گردن + 2nd-person suffix implicit), i.e. 'upon you'. سوگند و بند are the items that 'remain' (an oath and a bond/fetter), with و linking them.
segments.s020-g54: شوی is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of شدن 'to become' ('you will become/you shall be'), here predicting a negative fate: خوار 'despised/low'. و links the clauses; ماند is 3rd-person 'remains' and the poetic word order places پدرت (your father + possessive -ت) before the predicate ارجمند ('esteemed/ honoured'), i.e. 'and your father will remain honoured.'
segments.s020-g55: سر مرد تازی is a genitive phrase 'the head of the Arab man' (poetic omission of the ezāfe). به دام آورید is the past tense plural آوَردن with the idiom به دام آوردن 'to ensnare/capture'; آورید is 3rd-person plural past 'they brought/caused', so the clause says they ensnared/caught the Arab man.
segments.s020-g56: چنان شد که is a fixed phrase 'so it happened that / thus it came to pass that' (چنان 'so', شد 'happened/became', که introduces the result clause). فرمان او برگزید: فرمان 'command/order' with او 'his' (possessive), and برگزید is past 'chose/selected' (here idiomatically 'put into effect / carried out' the command). Word order is somewhat literary/elliptical but follows classical Persian patterns.

sentences.s020-s45: He set the Arabs' helmet on his head and meted out gains and losses to them.
sentences.s020-s1: There was a man in that age, a lancer among the plain's horsemen.
sentences.s020-s2: Respected by both the king and the good men, he maintained a cold demeanor out of fear of the sovereign.
sentences.s020-s3: Whose name was Mardas, an esteemed man; in justice and generosity he occupied the highest rank.
sentences.s020-s4: He had, for himself, milch quadrupeds; from each of them a thousand came to his place.
sentences.s020-s9: The world-seeker was called Zahhak; he was brave, light‑minded, and corrupt.
sentences.s020-s10: Wherever they called his steed 'Bevor', they wrote/placed such a name on its flank.
sentences.s020-s11: Wherever Bevor was counted among the champions, his name was spoken in the Dari tongue ten thousand (times).
sentences.s020-s12: From the Arabian steeds to golden praise, Bevor possessed that — the name they bore.
sentences.s020-s21: Time has spared this aged khwāja; you will remain for a long while under him.
sentences.s020-s22: Take this wealthy man's rank; it will befit you in the world in his day.
sentences.s020-s23: If you keep faith with this saying of mine, you will be a sovereign, even a king.
sentences.s020-s24: When Zahhāk heard (this), he pondered; his heart became full of sorrow because of his father's blood.
sentences.s020-s17: The kind‑hearted youth complied and carried out the order; just as he had commanded, he swore an oath.
sentences.s020-s18: That I will not tell your secret to anyone at all; from you I will hear whatever words you say.
sentences.s020-s19: He said to him, 'Besides you, who is a headman? What need is there to have anyone with you in the house?'
sentences.s020-s20: What use is a son who kills his father if he is like you? Someone ought to hear one piece of your counsel from me.
sentences.s020-s29: He asked, "Tell me this remedy — I will not be swayed at all by your counsel."
sentences.s020-s30: He said to him, "I will devise a remedy for you; I will lift your head toward the sun."
sentences.s020-s31: That king had, within his inner palace, a certain very delightful garden.
sentences.s020-s32: The noble night-attendant rose and made himself ready for worship.
sentences.s020-s41: He became an accomplice in his father's blood; I heard this tale from a wise man.
sentences.s020-s42: That if a son turns wicked, even if he is a male lion, he will not be brave regarding his father's blood.
sentences.s020-s43: But in its secrecy the tale is different; the secret for the inquirer is with the mother.
sentences.s020-s44: The base-born Zahhāk, the tyrant, by this stratagem seized the father's place.
sentences.s020-s37: He fell into the well and was shattered; that kind‑hearted, God‑worshipping man became humbled.
sentences.s020-s38: In both good and ill the king was a generous man; may a cold wind be upon the pampered son.
sentences.s020-s39: He raised him with both pampering and hardship; he was pleased with him and gave him treasure.
sentences.s020-s40: Thus his ill‑disposed, impudent son turned away from the path of justice and from his bonds.
sentences.s020-s13: Day and night you had two shares at the saddle — out of magnanimity, not out of hatred.
sentences.s020-s14: It was so bad that, one morning at dawn, Iblis came as if he were a well-wisher.
sentences.s020-s15: He carried away the chief's heart from the path of goodness; the young man gave ear to his words and entrusted himself to them.
sentences.s020-s16: He said to him, 'First I will have your pledge; then I will properly unfold my speech.'
sentences.s020-s5: That same milking-cow, in obedience; that same chosen Tazi mare.
sentences.s020-s6: Goats and ewes, rich in milk likewise, were given to the maidens (by the pious).
sentences.s020-s7: When someone was in need of a lion, at that request you lifted your hand (i.e. acted on the request).
sentences.s020-s8: There was not a single son of that pure‑hearted man who had even a small share of affection for me.
sentences.s020-s33: You sat with head and body hidden in the garden; the worshipper took the lamp with him.
sentences.s020-s34: He brought forth the devil's inverted snare; a certain deep well he dug in the road.
sentences.s020-s35: Then Iblis turned that deep well upside down, covered it with chaff, and hid the road.
sentences.s020-s36: Namjuy, the Arab chief, came by night and turned his face toward the garden.
sentences.s020-s25: He said to Iblis, "This is not fitting; speak no more, for this has no place in the matter."
sentences.s020-s26: He said to him, "If you can let this speech pass, you will be affected by my oath and pledge."
sentences.s020-s27: Let the oath and bond rest on your neck; you will become despised, and your father will remain honoured.
sentences.s020-s28: They brought the Arab man's head into a trap; thus it came about that he carried out his command.
